---
title: "Transformations"
description: "Stabilize variance by choosing a response transformation with Box-Cox."
categories: [Regression, Transformations]
---

::: {.callout-note title="Introduction"}
Use a Box-Cox plot to choose a response transformation when constant-variance or
normality assumptions are violated.
:::

```{r}
#| message: false

# read data
sales <- read.table("../../data/CH03PR17.txt", header=TRUE)

# view boxcox plot
gfit <- lm(Y ~ X, data=sales)
library(MASS)
boxcox(gfit)

# transform data
gfit_trfm <- lm(sqrt(Y) ~ X, data=sales)
summary(gfit_trfm)
```

## Answers

### Box-Cox Plot
The Box-Cox procedure suggests a lambda = .5 transformation would be
appropriate.

### Interpret Transformed Data
For each one-year increase, the expected change in square root of sales in
thousands is 1.07629.


## See also

- [Check Assumptions](check-assumptions.qmd)
- [Transformations](../experiments/transformations.qmd)

*Data: `CH03PR17.txt` — see [sources & provenance](../../data/README.md#regression).*
